coredata.dc:title: Advanced molecular recognition of 3-nitro-L-tyrosine: The use of zwitterion embedded molecularly imprinted mesoporous organosilica with sub-nanomolar sensitivity
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Youngdo
Lee, Jaeho
coredata.dc:description: Fabricating a state-of-the-art system capable of probing any chosen target molecule with a high degree of selectivity is the foremost objective of molecular recognition materials. In this paper, we developed a versatile target-probe utilizing zwitterion embedded molecularly imprinted mesoporous organosilica to fill the gap in our current capabilities. Graphene quantum dots were encapsulated as a signal transducer to prepare the fluorescent probe (NTIMO-zQ), and the concentration-dependent emission change was analyzed by adding 3-nitro-L-tyrosine (NT). The NTIMO-zQ showed an unprecedented degree of fluorescence quenching which also exhibited a sub-nanomolar sensitivity for NT; proving itself to be the most sensitive NT probe reported to date. By investigating the sigmoidal fitting of this quenching behavior, the selectivity performance can be quantitatively analyzed; and the resulting measurements are taken to determine the effective concentration (EC50) values with respect to NT. The NTIMO-zQ probe presents an extremely low EC50 with NT (9.7 nM) compared to several other NT analogues. The probe we have developed is both reproducible and repeatable with a satisfactory recovery rate (97–102%), and moreover, it exhibits suitably low detection limit (0.0129 nM).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecular imprinting
Mesoporous organosilica
Graphene quantum dot
Zwitterion
3-Nitro-L-tyrosine
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthetic characteristics of zwitterion-embedded MIMO probe
Structural characterization of quantum dot-encapsulated NTIMO-z probe
Sensitive molecular recognition performance of NTIMO-zQ probe
Comparison of selectivity performance of NTIMO-zQ probe
Preparation of 3-nitro-L-tyrosine-imprinted zwitterion embedded mesoporous organosilica nanoparticles (NTIMO-z)
Preparation of the graphene quantum dot-encapsulated NTIMO-z (NTIMO-zQ or zQ')

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Ing. R. Drumm (Leibniz-Institute for New Material, Saarbrücken, Germany), and Dr. J. Y. Hwang (KIST Jeonbuk, Republic of Korea) for critical measurements and services. This work was supported by the Basic Fund (11911) of KIST Europe.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sensitivity performance of NTIMO-zQ probe. (a) Fluorescence emission quenching profile of 3-nitro-L-tyrosine (NT) imprinted silica at various concentrations (from 0.1 nM to 100 μM). (b) Quenching profile of non-imprinted silica (NIMO-zQ) at different concentrations (from 1 μM to 100 μM).

Selectivity test of NTIMO-zQ probe. The quenching percent computed from the fluorescence emission profiles of NTIMO-zQ was plotted against the logarithm of each analyte concentration in the aqueous solution. The data were fitted by 3-parameter sigmoidal curve describing a binding performance. All measurements were independently repeated three times on each experimental concentration.
Schematic description on the fabrication of zwitterion embedded 3-nitro-L-tyrosine (NT)-imprinted mesoporous organosilica (NTIMO-zQ) probe.

head.ce:title: Advanced molecular recognition of 3-nitro-L-tyrosine: The use of zwitterion embedded molecularly imprinted mesoporous organosilica with sub-nanomolar sensitivity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biosensor Group, Korea Institute of Science and Technology Europe, Campus E7-1, 66123, Saarbrücken, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology Europe

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biosensor Group, Korea Institute of Science and Technology Europe, Campus E7-1, 66123, Saarbrucken, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fabricating a state-of-the-art system capable of probing any chosen target molecule with a high degree of selectivity is the foremost objective of molecular recognition materials. In this paper, we developed a versatile target-probe utilizing zwitterion embedded molecularly imprinted mesoporous organosilica to fill the gap in our current capabilities. Graphene quantum dots were encapsulated as a signal transducer to prepare the fluorescent probe (NTIMO-zQ), and the concentration-dependent emission change was analyzed by adding 3-nitro-L-tyrosine (NT). The NTIMO-zQ showed an unprecedented degree of fluorescence quenching which also exhibited a sub-nanomolar sensitivity for NT; proving itself to be the most sensitive NT probe reported to date. By investigating the sigmoidal fitting of this quenching behavior, the selectivity performance can be quantitatively analyzed; and the resulting measurements are taken to determine the effective concentration (EC50) values with respect to NT. The NTIMO-zQ probe presents an extremely low EC50 with NT (9.7 nM) compared to several other NT analogues. The probe we have developed is both reproducible and repeatable with a satisfactory recovery rate (97–102%), and moreover, it exhibits suitably low detection limit (0.0129 nM).

ce:section.ce:para: One of the major concerns when dealing with reactive nitrogen species derived from inflammatory cells is the mediation of the nitration of tyrosine to produce 3-nitro-L-tyrosine (NT) (Nagaraj et al., 2007). This process commonly results in a dramatic increase in NT levels in biological samples. Detection of increased levels of NT plays a critical role in interpreting a wide range of diseases such as atherosclerosis, osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, Alzheimer’s, cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, as well as various neurological diseases and psychiatric disorders (Gurung et al., 2018; Sandhu et al., 2003; Shiojiri et al., 2002; Butterfield et al., 2007; Shishehbor et al., 2003; Wang et al., 2004; Boje et al., 2004; Andreazza et al., 2010). As a clinically relevant biomarker of nitrosative stress, the ultralow content of NT in biological samples has been a prime mover for the development of sensitive, specific, and reliable methodologies. For example, high-performance liquid chromatography (LC) and gas chromatography (GC) equipped with mass spectrometry (MS) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) are the regularly preferred methods used to quantify NT levels (Chen et al., 2008; Frost et al., 2000; Khan et al., 1998). These very common and commercialized methods require however, careful sample storage and pretreatment procedures, costly laboratory equipment and well-trained personnel.

In this regard, molecularly imprinted fluorescent nanomaterials, the rigid polymeric matrices mimicking homogeneous binding sites used for target recognition, present the clear advantages of simple synthesis, low cost, non-destructivity, high sensitivity and rapid detection rates (Yang et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2016; Jalili et al., 2018). Despite significant progress having been made in the use of a number of target analytes, the molecular recognition properties as a fluorescent detection probe are still relatively unsatisfactory, and challenges persist in respect to the sensitivity and selectivity (Zhang et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016b, 2016a, 2018; Wang and Zhang, 2018; Yu et al., 2017; Kim et al., 2017; Zaidi et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019; Bagheri et al., 2019). Furthermore, most molecular imprinting-based fluorescent probes expressed their sensing mechanism via only photoinduced electron transfer processes which result in the application of the linear Stern-Volmer equation. However, this relatively simple chemical interaction based quenching mechanism makes it difficult to explain the presence of what are essentially, highly sensitive fluorescent probes. The first-order Stern-Volmer equation could not produce the desired results if the sensing mechanism is rooted in not only complicated non-covalent interactions but also in the chemical binding itself. Furthermore, there persists a general lack of research on this specific area (Medintz et al., 2003). This could be attributed to the quite similar fluorescence quenching models. More recently, however, we reported a highly sensitive MIMO-based fluorescent glyphosate probe, in which the imidazolium zwitterionic group-incorporated binding pockets are formed on the silica framework itself (Kim et al., 2019). The results of our sensitivity testing shows a linearity against the logarithm of the target concentration which is up to the sub-nanomolar level, implying that it cannot be fitted with a linear Stern-Volmer equation. Since this equation connotes the assumption that all signal transducers have the same condition, it could not explain a system that only includes the contributions of signals dependent upon the environment. Based on this difference in fluorescence quenching models, further exploration should be conducted in order to determine the exact quenching mechanism. This might achieve a facile strategy for optimizing MIMO-based fluorescent probes that involve fluorescence quenching behavior.

On the basis of the advantages by these MIMO-based probes, we aimed to develop a general tool for detecting the target without the need to alter the entire probe system or to engage in any chemical modification. To improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the probe, the zwitterionic group compound is employed as the target-binding silica precursor, and the molecular recognition performance is then evaluated. In this study, we report an advanced MIMO probe which retains not only a facile quantification of the target analyte but also improved rebinding properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic characteristics of zwitterion-embedded MIMO probe
ce:section.ce:para: The NT MIMO probe (NTIMO-zQ) was initially designed by the following procedure (Scheme 1 ). Our group added the imidazolium zwitterionic group-containing silica precursor to the target molecule upon the synthesis of MIMO. This has clearly demonstrated great utility in developing an improved glyphosate probe owing to the rebinding site containing a degree of zwitterionic moiety (Kim et al., 2019). Thus, we initially focused on the formation of a NT-rebinding pocket by synthesizing a different type of zwitterionic precursor bearing an ammonium group (z) as a functional silane monomer. We hypothesized that the molecular recognition performance is presumably enhanced by the change of the electronic state in charge separated non-bulky zwitterionic groups with target species, which may retain a specific strength of binding by complex formation. Following the sol-gel reaction of this monomer (z), the target (NT), and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) with a structure-directing agent (CTAB), we selectively removed the NT and CTAB simultaneously to yield the NTIMO-z. To confirm the successful synthesis of the NTIMO-z, the residual content of CTAB and NT in NTIMO-z was analyzed by means of FT-IR spectroscopy (Supporting Information, Fig. S1). The NT-embedded silica showed the bands at the range of 3000 to 2800 cm-1 which is assigned to the C–CH2 stretching vibration from the methylene chains of CTAB units. The characteristic bands appeared in the ranges of 1550-1490 and 1355-1315 cm-1 which stem from an aromatic NO2 group (Park et al., 2019). After the subsequent reflux under acidic conditions, all characteristic peaks disappeared in the spectrum of NTIMO-z, indicating that the mesoporous silica matrix had been formed with the imprinted cavities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of quantum dot-encapsulated NTIMO-z probe
ce:section.ce:para: The structure of the mesoporous silica nanoparticle was characterized by use of a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) and through Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area analysis. HRTEM images were obtained in order to characterize the morphology of NTIMO-z (Supporting Information, Fig. S2). The size of the nanoparticles was uniform (~50 nm). In addition, the size and arrangement of the mesoporous network was morphologically homogeneous, indicating that the mesoporous organosilica matrix had been formed successfully. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of NTIMO-z and zQ clearly showed that all the samples exhibited type-IV isotherms, which is characteristic of a mesoporous structure (Supporting Information, Fig. S3) (Yang et al., 1998). Meanwhile, NTIMO-zQ was found to have relatively lower BET surface areas (763 m2g-1) than the NTIMO-z (908 m2g-1). The decrease in the BET surface area was attributed to the encapsulation of graphene quantum dots (GQD) onto the mesopores of silica matrix, resulting in the occupation of mesopores. This result further demonstrated that the graphene quantum dots had been successfully incorporated into the porous framework of NTIMO-z.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive molecular recognition performance of NTIMO-zQ probe
ce:section.ce:para: The rebinding properties of NTIMO-zQ were initially investigated from their fluorescence emission profiles (Fig. 1 a). The spectra indicated that the specific binding accessibility of NT led to a wide range of responses (0.1 nM–100 μM) and large blue luminescent GQD emission decreases; 39.7% quenching at 10 nM NT. It was found that the lowest fluorescence quenching was observed at a concentration of 0.1 nM, lower than the least measurable dose of commercial ELISA kits (2.0 nM) (ELISA Kit, HK501). The spectra of non-imprinted silica (NIMO-zQ) are shown in Fig. 1b, which indicates only a few degrees of change on the whole range of NT concentrations. Since there are no binding sites on NIMO-zQ, this proves that the specific binding sites could have a sensitive molecular recognition property in regards to NT.

The kinetic rebinding experiments were conducted on NTIMO-zQ' containing green luminescent GQD in an aqueous system (Supporting Information, Fig. S4). Firstly, the kinetic rate (Raterebinding) was defined by the postulation that it would be determined by the rebinding capacity of a target molecule (NT) into a specific imprinted cavity (Equation (1)).
(1)
R a t e r e b i n d i n g
=
k
[ C ]
[ N T ]
where k is a rate constant, [C] is the total number of binding cavities formed on NTIMO-zQ' and [NT] represents the fixed concentration of 3-nitro-L tyrosine.

Since determining the concentration of binding cavities is somewhat difficult, we fixed [NT] as 20 mM, which is 200 times higher than a saturated concentration of the sensitivity result (100 μM). We could therefore hypothesize that it is sufficient to occupy all populations of binding cavities. The kinetic rate of Equation (1) can therefore be expressed as the following equation (Equation (2)).
(2)
R a t e r e b i n d i n g =
k '
[ C ]
,
k '
= k
[ NT ]

We discovered that the equation follows a pseudo first order reaction, and the experimental kinetic binding performance was investigated by fluorescent analysis in order to explore our postulation. As shown in Fig. 2 a, as time-passed, the fluorescence intensity weakened dramatically, regardless of the kind of GQDs, and the quenching ended rapidly within 5 min. This means that most of the rebinding kinetics are completed over a relatively the short time up to approximately 10 min. This working time is far lower than that of commercial ELISA kits (3.5 h). Based on the estimated plot of this time response spectra we could further estimate that it only takes approximately 3 min for the target molecules to rebind into a half of the total binding sites (R2 = 0.98, Fig. S4b). This finding demonstrated that our probe system can recognize NT rapidly, accurately, and efficiently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of selectivity performance of NTIMO-zQ probe
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the NTIMO-zQ probe is crucial for practical applications. We chose different competitive molecules against NT: L-tryptophan, L-phenylalanine, L-tyrosine, and glycine (Fig. 2). When a specific enzyme, chymotrypsin, acts on protein degradation, L-tryptophan, L-tyrosine, and L-phenylalanine are the main amino acid residues which are formed and they need to be detected along with the NT (Kahsai et al., 2014; Mendive-Tapia et al., 2015). Glycine is a very simple type of amino acid, and its chemical structure could also possibly fit into the cavities. Moreover, the nitration of tyrosine is thought to not only be linked to health and disease but also serve as biological markers in many other capacities (Souza et al., 2008). Accordingly, the capability of our probe to specifically recognize NT from these residues is required for the determination of NT in biological environment and samples; however, the current, commonly used techniques, such as gas or liquid chromatography, and ELISA, have presented a number of analytical challenges when it comes to quantifying NT accurately (Tsikas et al., 2014; Onorato et al., 1998).

Selectivity test was conducted by analyzing a variety of competitive analyte compositions in combination with the target molecule (NT). The results of this selectivity of L-tyrosine, L-tryptophan, L-phenylalanine, and glycine against NT are described in Fig. 2. We computed the amount of fluorescence quenching (Q%) at each different concentration by the following equation (Equation (3)).
(3)
Q% = 100 ×
{
1 −
( F /
F 0
)
}
wherein F and F 0 represent the fluorescence intensity in the presence and the absence of the analyte, respectively. Each Q% induced by the rebinding activity between NTIMO-zQ and the analyte was plotted against the logarithm of the molar concentration (nM) of the analyte. We also measured Q% of both extremely low and high concentration regions to obtain the final quenching curves. This proves that it provides good fit with a logistic function (R2 = 0.99, Equation (4)).
(4)
f
( x )
= Q
%
m a x
+
Q
%
m i n
− Q
%
m a x
1 +
( x /
x 0
)
p
where Q%
max and Q%
min are the maximum and minimum value of Q%, respectively. x is the concentration of analyte, x 0 refers to the concentration at the point of inflection (linear region) of the function, and p is the power factor.

According to the sigmoidal response characteristics, there are a number of terminologies that may be variously used to determine the degree of selectivity performance; namely, the effective concentration (EC20, EC50, and EC80). Note that this is the least concentration that is required to occupy a certain portion of the existing binding sites. We confirmed that NTIMO-zQ had more than 78 times lower EC50 for NT than for other analogues (Supporting Information, Table S1). This suggests strongly that our probe can be used to selectively recognize the target species (refer to the fourth column of Table S1). Moreover, our probe exhibited a much higher Q%
max value for NT compared to other compounds (refer to the first column of Table S1). This high degree of selectivity can even be retained in the presence of all interference analytes, added in equimolar concentrations with respect to NT concentration (Supporting Information, Fig. S5). Upon the addition of different contents of the mixture (10 nM - 10 μM), the emission profile of NTIMO-zQ was seen to exhibit a notable quenching effect; 31.2% quenching at 10 nM. Although there was a minor level of negative interference effect by interfering compounds on the selectivity of the probe, their effects were not significant and the degree of target recognition remained at a very high level.

The NTIMO-zQ probe was used to analyze the water samples spiked with known amounts of NT as listed in the first column of Supporting Information, Table S2. To exclude instrumental errors, we prepared three independent sets of the spiked concentration, and this was determined using the standard dilution method. The concentration found by the probe was determined using a linear region of the plot in Fig. 2. A linear fit was estimated with the calibration data between EC20 (0.75 nM) and EC80 (125 nM).

The fit equation (R2 = 0.99, Equation (5)) is
(5)
Q
( x )
= a .
log 10
x + b , a = 13.4 ± 0.150 , b = 25.7 ± 0.210
where Q(x) is the quenching amount (%), and x is the corresponding bulk concentration (nM) of the target.

The quenching amounts (%) were obtained under each independent set of concentrations, and the concentration with the probe was computed by the projection to x-axis interception. The results are shown in the second and third column in Table S1, where, the NTIMO-zQ probe shows satisfactory recovery between 97 and 102%. We further verified that the data sets by recovery test (red dots) completely cover the fitting line produced by the calibration data (black dots) between EC20 and EC80 (Supporting Information, Fig. S6). From this data set, a limit of detection of 0.0129 nM was calculated using the 3σb method, where σb is the blank standard deviation (n = 5). Our system has the potential to be used for tracing NT in water with a high degree of both accuracy and recovery, demonstrating a reliable limit of detection. This in turn leaves open the possibility of our probe’s suitability as a low-cost diagnostic kit for urine, plasma, environmental analysis, and other practical applications.

In summary, we surpass the challenge of high sensitivity and selectivity together by the use of a delicate zwitterion-based molecular recognition system capable of determining 3-nitro-L-tyrosine (NT). Through the optimization on a silane monomer containing a NT-binding zwitterionic functional group, a novel MIMO probe (NTIMO-zQ) showed unprecedent levels of sensitivity combined with high levels of selectivity. By taking advantage of investigating the lower and upper limits on the basis of fluorescence quenching profiles, we suggest using the sigmoidal response plot which provides an effective concentration (EC50) to evaluate the performance of molecular rebinding. The EC50 of NTIMO-zQ toward NT reached 9.7 nM, more than 78 times lower than that toward other structural analogues, which demonstrates its potential for a wide variety of practical sensing applications. The rebinding kinetic and recovery test results indicate that our probe can be used for the rapid tracing of NT in the water sample, providing an accurate means of screening for potential hazards related to NT levels. It is conceivable that this advanced constructive development of a MIMO-based probe can lead to major progress in molecular imprinting methodologies, which in turn, clearly highlight future directions for the use of molecular recognition platforms by researchers to develop other promising probing materials.

4-{dimethyl[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]azaniumyl}butane-1-sulfonate (z) were synthesized according to methods outlined in extant literature on this subject (Wang et al., 2015). 3-Nitro-L-tyrosine, L-tyrosine, glycine, L-tryptophan, L-phenylalanine, hexadecyl(trimethyl)azanium bromide (CTAB), and tetraethyl silicate (TEOS) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All chemicals were used without any further purification. All other solvents and reagents were of a chemically pure grade and were used as purchased.

FT-IR measurements were made on SHIMADZU IRSpirit with QATR-S. N2 uptake amounts were measured by means of a Belsorp-Max (BEL Japan, Inc.) apparatus. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained using a JEOL JEM 2100 operating at 200 kV. Fluorescent detections were performed on a PerkinElmer LS 45 luminescence spectrometer with a 10 mm quartz emission cuvette from Helma.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of 3-nitro-L-tyrosine-imprinted zwitterion embedded mesoporous organosilica nanoparticles (NTIMO-z)
ce:section.ce:para: Hexadecyl(trimethyl)azanium bromide (CTAB, 0.94 g, 2.6 mmol) was dissolved in de-ionized water (480 mL), and then an aqueous sodium hydroxide solution (1.0 M, 7.0 mL) was added. 3-Nitro-L-tyrosine (NT, 50 mg, 0.22 mmol) and z (76 mg, 0.22 mmol) were dissolved in de-ionized water and were then slowly added to the reaction mixture. Following this, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 5.1 g, 25 mmol) was added using a drop. The reaction was carried out at 70 °C for 3 h whilst stirring. The resultant product was filtered, washed with distilled water and acetone, and dried in vacuo at 60 °C. The product was then refluxed in a solution of HCl (35 wt%, 10 g), distilled water (50 g) and 1,4-dioxane (150 g) at 110 °C for 24 h. The resultant product (NTIMO-z) was then isolated by means of filtration, washed with distilled water and acetone, and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. Non-imprinted mesoporous organosilica (NIMO-z) were synthesized using the same procedure, the only difference being that no NT was added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the graphene quantum dot-encapsulated NTIMO-z (NTIMO-zQ or zQ')
ce:section.ce:para: Either blue luminescent graphene quantum dots (Q, 5 mL, 1 mg mL-1 in H2O, Sigma Aldrich) or green luminescent graphene quantum dot (Q', 5 mL, 1 mg mL-1 in H2O, Sigma Aldrich) were added to a suspension of NTIMO-z (100 mg mL-1) in H2O. The mixture (NTIMO-zQ or zQ') was stirred overnight and isolated by means of filtration, washed with distilled water and then dried in vacuo at 30 °C. NIMO-zQ was prepared in the same way the only difference being that NIMO-z was used instead of NTIMO-z.

All analytical solutions of 3-nitro-L-tyrosine and its competing compounds were dissolved in de-ionized water. The NTIMO-zQ/NIMO-zQ stock suspension (2 mg mL-1) was diluted using absolute ethanol. The concentrations of all analytes were prepared by the dilution of a stock solution (10 mM). In measuring both sensitivity and single/multi-components selectivity, the stock solution (2.0 μL) and different concentration of analytes (200 μL) were independently mixed and stirred in each vial for 5 min, and the fluorescence emission spectra were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The precise wavelengths where the maximum intensity was observed were 450 nm (blue) and 530 nm (green).

The NTIMO-zQ stock suspension (50 μL, 2 mg mL-1 in ethanol) was dispersed in an aqueous solution of 3-nitro-L-tyrosine (20 mM), and the maximum fluorescence intensity at λ = 450 nm was recorded at every time interval up to 60 min.

Youngdo Kim: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Jaeho Lee: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Software, Supervision, Visualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Ing. R. Drumm (Leibniz-Institute for New Material, Saarbrücken, Germany), and Dr. J. Y. Hwang (KIST Jeonbuk, Republic of Korea) for critical measurements and services. This work was supported by the Basic Fund (11911) of KIST Europe.
